title: Polluters Pay Climate Fund Act of 2025
short_title: Polluters Pay Climate Fund Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Polluters Pay Climate Fund Act of 2025 creates a huge tax on big oil and gas companies that pollute the air. The government will collect one trillion dollars from these companies based on how much harmful gas they put into the air between 2000 and 2023. All this money will go into a special fund to help when bad weather disasters happen, make communities stronger against storms and floods, and help people who are hurt most by climate change. The law still lets people sue these companies in court and lets states and cities make their own climate rules too.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Polluters Pay Climate Fund Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. Climate change is mostly caused by burning fossil fuels like coal, oil, and gas. It is an immediate and serious threat to American communities, the environment, and the economy. Severe effects of climate change are already happening in the United States. These include rising sea levels, higher temperatures, extreme weather, flooding, heat waves, loss of plants and animals, and other threats to natural areas. The federal government must work with states and local communities to develop and carry out protective measures. These measures will fight the harmful effects of climate change, protect communities, and help them become stronger against extreme weather. The government response must especially protect communities that are most at risk from climate change effects. This includes communities of color, low income communities, and Tribal and Indigenous communities. These communities often have not received enough public investment in the past and are more likely to be harmed by fossil fuel pollution. The protective measures needed to respond to climate change in the United States will require trillions of dollars of new investment during the ten years after this law is passed. Climate change related extreme weather events cost the United States at least one hundred fifty billion dollars each year. These events hurt underserved and overburdened communities the most, according to the Fifth National Climate Assessment. The one hundred billion dollars each year that fossil fuel companies will pay into the Polluters Pay Climate Fund created by this law is only a small part of the total cost to the federal government to respond to climate change related extreme weather and make needed climate change preparation and strength building investments. Scientific research can now determine very accurately how much carbon dioxide was released into the air by the operations and products of specific fossil fuel companies. This research is what helps create the formulas to determine carbon dioxide emissions that are used in the changes made by this law. The fossil fuel industry has known since before the year two thousand that their product plays a central role in causing climate change. The fossil fuel industry must now increase how much they contribute to government spending to protect the nation from climate disaster. This law and the fees under the changes made by this law are not meant to determine fault or to affect the ability of any person or other government to hold polluters responsible for harms they caused.

sections.title: Section 3  Tax Relating to Current Stock of Greenhouse Gas Emissions
sections.text: This section adds a new tax to the federal tax code. Each company that must pay this tax (called an assessable person) must pay the Secretary of the Treasury by September thirtieth twenty twenty six. The tax amount for each company is calculated based on how much carbon dioxide they are responsible for compared to all other companies that must pay this tax. The total amount collected from all companies will be one trillion dollars. To figure out each company's share, the Secretary will look at how much carbon dioxide emissions can be traced to that company's products. Only companies responsible for more than one billion metric tons of covered carbon dioxide emissions have to pay this tax. The Secretary can adjust the amount if a company shows that some of their emissions were already counted for another company. A company must pay this tax if they are a United States company or do business in the United States during the period from when this law is passed until December thirty first twenty twenty five. The company must have been in the business of either extracting fossil fuels or refining crude oil during some part of the covered period. The covered period is from January first two thousand to December thirty first twenty twenty three. Companies that are controlled by the same owners are treated as one company for this tax. If multiple people are responsible for paying the tax for one company, they are all responsible for the full amount. The tax defines covered carbon dioxide emissions as the total amount of carbon dioxide released into the air during the covered period because a company was extracting fossil fuels or refining crude oil. Fossil fuel means coal, crude oil, and fuel gases. Coal includes anthracite, bituminous, subbituminous, and lignite coal. Crude oil means oil or petroleum of any kind including bitumen, oil sands, heavy oil, conventional and unconventional oil, shale oil, natural gas liquids, condensates, and related fossil fuel liquids. Fuel gases means natural gas, associated natural gas, conventional and unconventional gas, shale gas, and related methane gas production. To determine carbon dioxide emissions, the law sets specific amounts. For every one million pounds of coal, nine hundred forty two and five tenths metric tons of carbon dioxide are counted as released. For every one million barrels of crude oil, four hundred thirty two thousand one hundred eighty metric tons of carbon dioxide are counted as released. For every one billion cubic feet of fuel gases, fifty four thousand four hundred forty metric tons of carbon dioxide are counted as released. Companies can choose to pay this tax in nine yearly payments instead of all at once. The first payment would be twenty percent of the total tax. Each of the following eight payments would be ten percent of the total tax. If a company chooses to pay in installments, the first payment is due on September thirtieth twenty twenty six. Each following payment is due on the same date in following years. If a company fails to pay on time, sells most of their assets, stops doing business, or faces similar problems, all remaining payments become due immediately. The Secretary of the Treasury must create rules within eighteen months after this law is passed to carry out this section. Companies cannot deduct this tax as a business expense on their federal tax returns.

sections.title: Section 4  Polluter Pays Climate Change Fund
sections.text: This section creates a special fund in the United States Treasury called the Polluters Pay Climate Fund. All the money collected from the tax in Section three goes into this fund. The money in the fund can be used, as approved by Congress through spending bills, for the purposes described in this section. The Secretary of the Treasury, working with the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency and heads of other relevant agencies, will use the money in the fund for a comprehensive and fair federal response to climate change impacts. The money will go toward investments in climate resilience, adaptation, disaster response, and environmental justice. This includes climate related disaster recovery and prevention support, climate change adaptation support through climate and disaster planning help, funding for climate resilient infrastructure, and improved climate and extreme weather prediction abilities. It also includes programs that make energy systems more resilient to climate change through energy efficiency, grid resilience, and distributed electricity generation programs. The fund will support programs that make the food system more resilient to climate change by supporting climate resilient farming practices. It will fund programs that make the transportation system more resilient to climate change through planning and climate change adaptation support. The money will go toward programs that make ecosystems more resilient to climate change through conservation, restoration, and wildfire management activities. It will support climate related public health programs, including efforts to address extreme heat. The fund will also support programs that make drinking water and stormwater infrastructure more resilient to climate change. Each year, the Secretary must use at least fifteen billion dollars from the fund to give to the Federal Emergency Management Agency for response and resilience programs that address climate related disasters like hurricanes, flooding, extreme heat, and wildfires. Of this amount, at least three billion dollars must be used for the Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities program. Each year, the Secretary must also use at least six billion dollars from the fund for grants and technical assistance under section one hundred thirty eight of the Clean Air Act. The Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency can decide the right amounts to use for those grants and technical assistance. Of all the money taken from the fund each year, forty percent must be used for investments that help environmental justice communities. Environmental justice communities are communities with many people of color, low income communities, or Tribal and Indigenous communities that experience or are at risk of experiencing worse health or environmental effects compared to other communities. For money from the fund beyond the required amounts, the Secretary will work with the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency and heads of other relevant agencies to create selection criteria. These criteria will give the highest priority to projects or activities that are most effective at achieving the purposes described in this section.

sections.title: Section 5  Availability of Remedies
sections.text: Nothing in this law or the changes it makes removes any person's responsibility under common law or under any state or federal law. Nothing in this law, the changes it makes, the Clean Air Act, or federal common law prevents, replaces, or limits any right or remedy that any person, state, local government, or Tribal government has under any state or local law including common law. This applies to claims about lying about the effects of fossil fuel on climate change, damage or injury from the role of fossil fuel in contributing to climate change, or failure to avoid damage or injury related to climate change. These claims include lawsuits for nuisance, trespass, design defect, negligence, failure to warn, or deceptive or unfair practices. They also include claims asking for court orders, declarations, money, or other relief. Nothing in this law requires paying back any money from the Polluters Pay Climate Fund that was used according to section four even if a court awards damages in any lawsuit about the causes of action or claims described above. The law also does not allow using any such funds as evidence in such lawsuits or to reduce any court awarded damages in such lawsuits.

sections.title: Section 6  Non Preemption of Authorities
sections.text: Nothing in this law or the changes it makes prevents or overrides any state or local law, regulation, policy, or program. This includes laws, regulations, policies, and programs that limit, set, or enforce standards for greenhouse gas emissions. It also includes those that monitor, report, and keep records of greenhouse gas emissions. The law does not override state or local measures that provide cost recovery for climate adaptation, mitigation, or resilience. It also does not override state or local measures that conduct or support investigations.
